content:
10 min read
Just now

--

A tiny dataset from a failed video game shortcut shows something surprisingly universal: progress often becomes invisible right before it becomes obvious.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Progress rarely feels like progress while it is happening.
When we learn a difficult skill, we often imagine improvement as a smooth upward line: each attempt should be slightly better than the previous one. But real learning is usually much noisier. It includes early progress, sudden drops, long plateaus, hidden improvements, and then a breakthrough that feels almost random.
This small experiment started with a simple video game shortcut.
The task was easy to describe but hard to execute: perform three correct jumps in a row.
I recorded every attempt using two symbols:
O — successful jump
X — failed jump/death
A complete success would therefore look like this:
OOOThe full dataset contained 41 attempts:
OX
X
OX
OX
X
OX
OOX
OX
X
X
X
OX
OX
OX
OX
OOX
X
X
X
X
X
OOX
X
OX
X
X
OX
X
X
OOX
X
OX
OOX
X
X
OOX
OX
OOX
OOX
X
OOOThe first full success happened on attempt 41.
At first glance, this looks like a story of repeated failure.
But when the attempts are analyzed as a learning process, the pattern becomes much more interesting.

1. Not All Failures Are Equal
To measure progress, I scored each attempt by how far it got into the three-jump sequence:
X = 0 points — failed on the first jump
OX = 1 point — completed one jump, failed on the second
OOX = 2 points — completed two jumps, failed on the third
OOO = 3 points — completed the full sequenceAcross all 41 attempts, the distribution was:
X : 19 attempts — 46.3%
OX : 13 attempts — 31.7%
OOX : 8 attempts — 19.5%
OOO : 1 attempt — 2.4%Across all attempts, there were 72 recorded jump events:
Total attempts: 41
Total jump events: 72
Successful jumps: 32
Failed jumps: 40
Overall per-jump success: 44.4%
Full successful runs: 1If we only look at complete success, the story is simple:
40 failed attempts, then 1 success.
But that view is too shallow.
It treats all failures as equal.
They were not equal.
Failing immediately on the first jump is very different from failing on the third jump after completing the first two. The real learning signal is not only whether I succeeded, but how far into the sequence my failures moved.

2. Raw Attempts and Rolling Performance
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The blue line shows each attempt. The orange line shows the trailing 5-attempt average. The curve is noisy: early progress, visible regression, a long unstable plateau, and a late breakthrough.
This chart shows two things at once.
The individual attempts are chaotic. Some good attempts appear early, then disappear. There are sudden drops. There are long stretches where it looks like nothing is improving.
But the rolling average reveals the larger shape:

• Early progress — several OX attempts appear quickly, and the first OOX happens on attempt 7.

• Plateau and regression — the middle section contains long stretches of X, including five first-jump failures in a row.

• Late improvement — near the end, OOX attempts become much more frequent, and the first OOO finally appears.
This is important because the learning curve is not smooth.
I did not simply get a little better on every attempt.
Instead, performance moved in waves:
discovery → collapse → adjustment → plateau → breakthrough
That is much closer to how real skill acquisition often feels.

3. Why the Beginning Looked Good
The beginning of the dataset was not terrible:
OX
X
OX
OX
X
OX
OOXBy attempt 7, I had already reached the third jump once.
This is typical of early learning.
At the start, the brain is usually correcting large, obvious errors:

• Where to stand;

• When to press the button;

• What angle to use;

• How much movement input is needed;

• What a “good” first jump feels like.
Because these are big corrections, early learning can feel fast. A single discovery can immediately turn an X into an OX, or an OX into an OOX.
But early progress is often unstable.
It means the learner has found something useful.
It does not mean the skill is already reliable.
That is exactly what happened here.
The first OOX on attempt 7 was important, but it did not immediately lead to consistent success. Instead, the process entered a plateau.

4. The Plateau: When Average Performance Hides Progress
For Medium, this table is best shown as a compact code block rather than a native table, because Medium tables can be unreliable.
Block X OX OOX OOO Avg score
-------------------------------------------------------
Early discovery 1–10 4 5 1 0 0.70
Plateau 1 11–20 5 4 1 0 0.60
Plateau 2 21–30 6 2 2 0 0.60
Breakthrough 31–41 4 2 4 1 1.18This table is more useful than a simple bar chart of average scores.
Why?
Because the average alone hides the structure of the failures.
Attempts 11–20 and 21–30 both have the same average score:
0.60
At first glance, that suggests no improvement.
But the distribution changed.
In attempts 11–20, there was only one OOX.
In attempts 21–30, there were two OOX attempts.
The average score stayed flat, but the failures started moving later into the sequence.
That is a subtle but important sign of learning.
A plateau in the average score can hide progress in the structure of the failures.

5. Near-Successes Are the Strongest Learning Signal
For this task, a “near-success” means reaching the third jump:
OOX or OOOThis is a better learning signal than complete success alone, because full success happened only once, at the very end.
If we only track OOO, the first 40 attempts look like zero progress.
But if we track near-successes, the learning curve becomes visible.
The near-success rate changed like this:
Attempts 1–10: 1 / 10 = 10%
Attempts 11–20: 1 / 10 = 10%
Attempts 21–30: 2 / 10 = 20%
Attempts 31–41: 5 / 11 = 45%This is the strongest evidence of learning in the dataset.
The final block was not just lucky.
I was reaching the third jump much more often before the first full success appeared.
Near-success rate increased from 10% in the early blocks to 45% in the final block.
That means the final OOO was not an isolated miracle.
It was the visible result of a distribution that had already shifted toward better performance.

6. The Bottleneck Moved
Another useful way to analyze the attempts is to look at conditional progress through the three jumps.
Block Jump 1 completed Jump 2 after Jump 1 Jump 3 after Jump 2
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1–10 60% 17% 0%
11–20 50% 20% 0%
21–30 40% 50% 0%
31–41 64% 71% 20%This is one of the most important parts of the whole analysis.
The bottleneck moved.
At first, I often failed very early.
Later, I more often survived the first and second jumps and failed near the end.
That means the errors were not disappearing randomly. They were moving deeper into the task.
That is one of the clearest signs of learning.
A beginner fails at the beginning.
An improving learner fails later.
An expert fails less often, but when they fail, it is usually at the hardest remaining point.
In this dataset, the third jump became the final bottleneck.
Once the first two jumps became more stable, there was enough consistency to finally complete the whole sequence.

7. The Ideal Learning Curve vs. What Actually Happened
A theoretical learning curve is often shown as a smooth S-shaped curve.
At the beginning, progress is slow because the learner is still discovering the basics. Then improvement accelerates after enough patterns are recognized. Finally, performance starts to level off as the learner approaches a practical ceiling.
That is the clean version of learning.
But real learning rarely feels clean.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The blue line shows the actual trailing 5-attempt average. The solid orange line is a stylized S-shaped learning curve used as a conceptual reference, not a fitted statistical model. The dashed green line is a descriptive trend line based on the actual rolling average.
The solid orange curve represents how learning is often imagined: smooth, gradual, and directionally obvious.
The blue line shows what actually happened in the recorded attempts.
It is much messier.
There is early progress, then a drop, then an unstable plateau, then a late rise. The actual data does not follow the theoretical curve attempt by attempt. It moves around it, falls below it, recovers, and only near the end starts to bend upward clearly.
That is the important point.
The theory is not wrong. It is just too clean.
Real learning is noisy because every attempt is affected by many small variables:

• Timing;

• Attention;

• Stress;

• Fatigue;

• Overcorrection;

• Changing strategy;

• Random execution error.
The dashed trend line helps reveal the larger pattern inside the noise.
It shows that even though the middle of the practice session looked bad, the overall direction eventually turned upward. The progress was not obvious from any single attempt, but it became visible when the attempts were smoothed and viewed as a sequence.
This is why plateaus can be misleading.
During the plateau, the learner may feel stuck. But underneath the surface, the brain may still be refining timing, reducing uncertainty, and turning separate actions into a more automatic sequence.
In this dataset, the late rise was not just one lucky OOO.
It was preceded by more frequent OOX attempts, meaning the player was already reaching the final stage more often. The breakthrough became visible only after enough hidden adjustments accumulated.
So the real lesson from this graph is not:
My data perfectly follows the theoretical learning curve.
Instead, it is:
Real learning is noisy, but the hidden trend can still bend upward long before success becomes obvious.

8. Does This Prove the Learning Curve?
Strictly speaking, no.
This is a small observational dataset:
one player, one task, 41 attempts.
It is not a controlled experiment, and it cannot prove a universal law of learning.
The correlation between attempt number and raw attempt score is only weakly positive. That is expected because the data is small, noisy, and discrete.
But the dataset strongly agrees with a realistic view of skill acquisition.
Learning theory expectation What the data shows
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Early gains can appear quickly Several OX attempts and one early OOX
Plateaus are common Flat or worse average performance in the middle
Improvement can be hidden Average score stayed flat while OOX increased
Failures shift later with skill Later attempts more often reached jump 3
Breakthroughs follow hidden progress Final OOO came after near-successes clusteredSo the data does not “prove” the theory in a strict scientific sense.
But it illustrates it extremely well.
The most important lesson is that progress was not best measured by success alone.
It was better measured by how the failures changed.

9. What Is Especially Interesting in This Dataset
The first near-success was not random noise
Attempt 7 was the first OOX.
At that moment, it could have looked like luck.
But later, especially in the final block, OOX appeared repeatedly.
That suggests attempt 7 was not just a random event. It was an early signal that part of the skill was already forming.
The plateau was not empty
The middle section looked frustrating.
There were many X attempts, including a stretch of five first-jump failures in a row.
But the plateau was not meaningless.
During this phase, I was likely trying to connect separate actions into a sequence.
That is harder than executing one jump in isolation.
The task changed from:
Do one jump correctly.to:
Do jump 1, prepare jump 2, keep rhythm, control stress, and execute jump 3.That extra complexity can temporarily make performance look worse, even while learning continues underneath.
The breakthrough was preceded by clustering
The attempts that reached the t
